output.summary:
Hungary's 6- and 8-year gimnázium tracks select students at age 10-12 through centralized entrance exams, producing an 85% share of selection-capable schools versus a 43% OECD average, extreme between-school variance (71%), and stark compositional imbalance (14% advantaged versus 1% deprived among top readers). Six transformation paths span a spectrum from inaction to structural abolition, and the choice hinges on distinguishing what is empirically known from what values are being weighed.

Status quo with monitoring (T1) changes nothing structurally; existing PISA/ABC reporting infrastructure continues generating equity data without a policy-correction mechanism attached. Evidence is strong that this infrastructure has historically not translated into action, so this path's main risk is not technical failure but legitimacy erosion: ministerial acknowledgment of segregation paired with no corrective step widens public distrust while demographic contraction concentrates elite tracks further in urban centers.

The two most structurally ambitious options attack the selection age itself. Delaying first selection to 14-16 (T2) and a comprehensive de-tracking pilot (T6) both target the mechanism most directly linked in comparative literature to inequality of opportunity: removing pre-adolescent sorting points. Poland's 1999 reform is the closest comparator, showing measured PISA and labor-market gains (3pp employment, 4% earnings), but Poland's reform was substantially reversed after 2016

violations: summary must carry at least one named mechanism from: big-fish, little-pond, self-concept, labeling, goal orientation, self-determination, motivation
